title: Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita, 2023

Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between economic output and health outcomes for nations in 2023, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against the disease burden from non-communicable diseases on the vertical axis. The vertical axis measures Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people, ranging from 0 to 50,000, while the horizontal axis spans from $2,000 to $100,000 in international dollars at 2021 prices. Circle size corresponds to population, with a reference scale showing markers for 600 million and 1.4 billion people. The data points form a distinct downward-sloping curve, showing that countries with lower GDP per capita generally experience higher rates of non-communicable disease burden. At the upper end of the disease burden scale, Nauru sits near 50,000 DALYs per 100,000, followed by the Marshall Islands and Solomon Islands in the 40,000 range. Other nations with high burdens relative to their income include Myanmar, Haiti, and Egypt, all positioned above the 30,000 mark. In the middle of the distribution, countries like India, Indonesia, and Vietnam cluster between 20,000 and 25,000 DALYs, with India and Indonesia represented by large circles indicating their substantial populations. Moving toward the right side of the chart, wealthier nations such as the United States, Germany, and Japan show lower disease burdens, generally falling between 15,000 and 25,000 DALYs. Singapore appears at the far right with a GDP per capita approaching $100,000 and a disease burden near 15,000. The color-coded legend identifies regional groupings, with African nations in purple, Asian nations in teal, and European nations in blue, showing that the inverse relationship between GDP and disease burden holds across these geographic categories. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time. The data shows a consistent pattern where higher GDP per capita coincides with lower disability-adjusted life years lost to non-communicable diseases.
